Hey guys, JB here. These weekend motivations are really about just giving you cool things to think about. Food for thought. Have a great day and let's do some serious butt kicking on Monday. All right? Take care. I have a great story. It's back when I was training myself to fly helicopters. And to get a helicopter license, you have to be able to land it without an engine. In other words, if the engine goes out, it's going to be able to land the thing without killing yourself. There's a procedure for that called auto rotation. What basically you do is, if you lose power at, let's say, 2,000 feet, you spool down the engine or it spools that itself, right? And you go into a 45 degree glide down. You don't go nuts, right? And then when you get about 50 feet off the ground, you pull back on the stick, you push down on the right pedal. And what happens is the thing flares, the nose flares, and you start losing the altitude, the energy, so it slows down. And at the last second, 5, 10 feet off the ground, you push it back down and it glides and it lands like a plane. Even if it has skids, it will glide to a perfect landing. Now the key here, though, is that when you are making your descent and you get down to that 50 foot mark and you pull back on the stick, what happens is to an untrained person is the panic sets in. You're like, oh my god, I'm going to crash. Oh my god. And you look down and what do you do? You crash into the ground. That's what happens. So when I was taking lessons, the pilot actually went through this. He goes, listen, here's what's going to happen. The first time you try this, it's just human nature. You're going to look at what you're afraid of and you're going to tend to crash right into the ground. He goes, but please try to resist that urge. And instead of doing that, look ahead. Look where you want to go. Look at your landing spot. 50 feet off the ground. And you're going to crash into the ground. Again, look where you want to go. Look at your landing spot. 50 yards ahead. And if you do that naturally, your body will take you in that direction. So I was like, all right, well, that sounds pretty good. I'm a self-development guy. Even back then when I was in the market, because I was training people on motivation, I said, I can buy into that whole idea that you don't want to focus on what you're afraid of. Focus where you want to go. So there you go. Here's what happens. He takes me up. We go up to altitude, right? Get to 2,000. He goes, you're ready? I said, yep, I'm ready. He goes, OK. He spools down the engine. The thing starts diving. I'm like, no problem, no problem. I go into my little control 45 degree dive. I'm using my left pedal here to keep it stable. We get to that 50 foot walk. I pull back on the stick slowly. We get to 40 feet, 30 feet. I'm like, holy shit. And I literally can't stop myself from looking in. And then he starts diving. And he grabs the stick. He pulls it back. He had his own stick, right? He goes, see? I told you, he takes it to a perfect landing, right? Crazy. I can't believe it. I knew I shouldn't do it, but I still did it. He goes, all right, just practice, do it again. We go back up a second time. Get to 2,000 feet. We do a pattern around the airport, right? Turns off the engine, 2,000 feet. Starts to dive. I go into my control dive, left pedal, 45 degrees. We get to 50 feet. I'm saying, this is not doing something. I'm not going to do it. They pull back on the stick, 50 feet, 40, 35. I'm like, oh, shit. And I start looking down again. And I start crashing again. He grabs the stick, pulls it back, right? And we go to a purpling. And now I'm like, mind boggled. He goes, it's all right. You're going to get it. Just to get it. Just keep doing this. It's a matter of just training yourself to overcome this just natural tendency to look at what you're afraid of. That's what people do. We go up the third time. We go to 2,000 feet. Turns it off, all right? Things start diving. I start nose diving down, 45 degree angle. Get there, 50 feet. I pull back on the stick, start bleeding off energy. And I said, this time, I don't look down. I look straight ahead. And sure enough, it goes to a smooth 10 feet. Stops glides to a perfect landing. I conditioned a new pattern after the third time. You see how powerful? As to me, that was something, whoa. I was like, even though I knew that I shouldn't look down, I still had that tendency to look down. We must avoid looking at what we're afraid of focusing that. Because we always will go in the direction of the focus.
